concept: Acute / Having a Recent, Rapid, or Short Clinical Course
description: Recent, rapid, or short in its clinical course, describing time and nothing else. The same condition can be acute and mild or acute and severe, because how fast something arrived says nothing about how much it hurts; that is 'pelaku' (severity), a separate word for a separate question.
sound_symbolism: A crisp velar closure releases 'ka' into broad 'a'. Stressed 'the' changes the hard contact into fine dental breath and balanced 'e'. Final 'o' stands alone across a clear hiatus. One round open beat remains after the fricative has faded.
grammatical_notes: An adjective: 'ha kaworu katheo nai.' (This injury is acute.) State the relevant onset and time scale where they matter. The quality-noun rule supplies acute status. Distinct from 'nurawi' (chronic), 'pelaku' (severity), 'pukea' (danger), and 'tiso' (sharp).

pillars.peace-linguistics-practices: Separating course from severity and danger prevents an acute label from becoming an unexplained urgency claim.

tags.cognition: a clinical classification under a stated time scale
tags.temporal: a recent, rapid, or comparatively short course
